ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for sweet potato sago dessert. Closest parent is tapioca pearl food product (FOODON_03311737), which covers sago/tapioca pearl-based preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Sweet dishes and desserts) is the closest parent category. No specific code exists for Thai sweet potato sago dessert in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for sweet potato sago dessert or sufficiently close Thai dessert category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one bowl of sweet potato sago dessert as typically served in Thai dessert shops and restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component ingredients: sago pearls (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169717), purple sweet potato (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168482), and coconut milk (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169885); proportions based on standard Thai sago dessert recipe composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for sweet potato sago dessert exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of component ingredients using a representative recipe: approximately 35% sago pearls (cooked), 30% purple sweet potato (cooked), 30% coconut milk, and 5% sugar/salt/pandan. Per-100g values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for each component and blended proportionally.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI based on analogous foods: sago pudding (GI ~66, University of Sydney GI Database) and sweet potato boiled (GI ~63, University of Sydney GI Database); weighted blend reflecting recipe composition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet potato sago dessert. GI estimated at 66 based on a weighted blend of: sago pudding (GI 66, University of Sydney GI Database, tested food) contributing approximately 35% of carbohydrate load, and boiled sweet potato (GI 63, University of Sydney GI Database) contributing approximately 30% of carbohydrate load, with coconut milk contributing negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (66 × 45.5) / 100 ≈ 30.0 g, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 18.2 × 2.5 = 45.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~28 mg per 100 g; ~70 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Sweet potato sago is low in sodium (~70 mg per 250 g serving), making it generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though the moderate sugar content warrants attention in those with concurrent metabolic concerns.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~66) and high glycemic load per serving (~30 g GL)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a medium GI of approximately 66 and a glycemic load of ~30 per 250 g serving, sweet potato sago can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise and should be consumed in small portions or avoided by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who are managing glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.2 g saturated fat per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk base contributes approximately 4.2 g of saturated fat per 100 g (~10.5 g per serving), which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Potato Sago (สาคูมันเทศ, Saku Man Thet) is a traditional Thai dessert made by simmering translucent sago pearls — derived from tapioca starch — in sweetened coconut milk, combined with tender chunks of purple sweet potato. The dessert is characterised by a creamy, mildly sweet coconut flavour, a soft and slightly chewy texture from the sago pearls, and the earthy sweetness of purple sweet potato, which also imparts a vivid violet hue to the dish. It is commonly served warm or at room temperature, sometimes garnished with pandan-infused coconut cream. In Singapore, this dessert is offered at Thai restaurants and dessert stalls, including establishments such as Saap Saap Thai. Nutritionally, sweet potato sago is a moderate-energy dessert (~118 kcal per 100 g) with a significant carbohydrate load (~18 g per 100 g) and notable saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.2 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 66 (medium), and the glycemic load per standard 250 g serving is approximately 30, making it a high-GL food that warrants caution for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal-suitable. Purple sweet potato contributes beta-carotene, anthocyanins, potassium, and vitamin C, while coconut milk provides medium-chain triglycerides. Clinically, the high glycemic load and saturated fat content are the primary concerns for metabolic and cardiovascular health.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for sweet potato sago dessert was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data using a representative recipe proportion. GI is estimated from analogous foods (sago pudding and boiled sweet potato) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the recipe proportions are consistent with standard Thai sago dessert formulations.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data (sago pearls FDC 169717, purple sweet potato FDC 168482, coconut milk FDC 169885) using approximate recipe proportions (35% cooked sago, 30% cooked purple sweet potato, 30% coconut milk, 5% sugar/salt/pandan). Direct laboratory analysis of this dish is not available in any approved primary source. 2. GI is an analogous estimate based on sago pudding (GI 66) and boiled sweet potato (GI 63) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dessert containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as no vitamin D-contributing ingredients are present; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as all ingredients are plant-derived; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from coconut milk and sweet potato USDA component data. 5. The input record description mentions mango, but the dish name and field dataing reference sweet potato sago specifically; mango is not included in the nutrient calculation as it is not a confirmed standard ingredient of this dish variant. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input (Pad Thai, Thai Noodle) are not relevant to this dessert and have been disregarded. 7. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for estimation and cannot be reliably derived from the blended component approach; these are documented as not calculable from available approved sources.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from menu-style 'VS2 Sweet Potato Sago' to canonical 'Sweet Potato Sago'.
Added Thai local name สาคูมันเทศ and romanized form Saku Man Thet to satisfy G6 Thai cuisine mandatory language requirement.
Estimated per_100g nutrients from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (sago pearls, purple sweet potato, coconut milk) using representative recipe proportions.
Estimated GI at 66 from analogous foods (sago pudding GI 66, boiled sweet potato GI 63) in University of Sydney GI Database.
Calculated glycemic load per 250 g serving as 30.0.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Documented all zero values (vitamin_b12, vitamin_d, trans_fat, cholesterol) with 'confirmed zero' justification in notes.
Documented all -1 sentinel values as not calculable from available approved sources in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Disregarded irrelevant cuisine_tags (Pad Thai, Thai Noodle) from input.
Set food_group to 'Desserts' as appropriate for this Thai sweet dessert.
